elements.sent-2411-002: Purpose: Automatic segmentation of brain tumors in intra-operative ultrasound (iUS) images could facilitate localization of tumor tissue during resection surgery.
elements.sent-2411-003: The lack of large annotated datasets limits the current models performances.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we investigate the use of tumor annotations in pre-operative MRI images, which are more easily accessible than annotations in iUS images, for training of deep learning models for iUS brain tumor segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-005: Methods: We used 180 annotated pre-operative MRI images with corresponding unannotated iUS images, and 29 annotated iUS images.
elements.sent-2411-006: Image registration was performed to transfer the MRI annotations to the corresponding iUS images before training models with the nnU-Net framework.
elements.sent-2411-007: To validate the use of MRI labels, the models were compared to a model trained with only US annotated tumors, and a model with both US and MRI annotated tumors.
elements.sent-2411-008: In addition, the results were compared to annotations validated by an expert neurosurgeon on the same test set to measure inter-observer variability.
elements.sent-2411-009: Results: The results showed similar performance for a model trained with only MRI annotated tumors, compared to a model trained with only US annotated tumors.
elements.sent-2411-010: The model trained using both modalities obtained slightly better results with an average Dice score of 0.62, where external expert annotations achieved a score of 0.67.
elements.sent-2411-011: The results also showed that the deep learning models were comparable to expert annotation for larger tumors ($>$ 200 mm$^2$), but perform clearly worse for smaller tumors ($<$ 200 mm$^2$).
elements.sent-2411-012: Conclusion: MRI tumor annotations can be used as a substitute for US tumor annotations to train a deep learning model for automatic brain tumor segmentation in intra-operative ultrasound images.
elements.sent-2411-013: Small tumors is a limitation for the current models and will be the focus of future work.
elements.sent-2411-014: The main models are available here: \url{https://github.com/mathildefaanes/us_brain_tumor_segmentation/tree/main .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The most frequent group of primary brain tumors are gliomas arising from glial cells.
elements.sent-2411-016: Gliomas are categorized into subtypes based on histopathological characteristics and molecular markers associated with their aggressiveness \cite{louis_2021_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The tumor cells tend to infiltrate the normal brain tissue, creating a diffuse border and making them largely incurable \cite{delgado-lopez_diffuse_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, treatment can prolong life and improve or at least prolong quality of life and brain functions \cite{kheirollahi_brain_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Surgical resection is often the preferred primary treatment option, where studies have shown that the extent of tumor resection is linked to prolonged survival \cite{hentschel_optimizing_2003, sanai_glioma_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Nevertheless, removing healthy surrounding tissue in this process could impact the function of the patient negatively \cite{sastry_applications_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Precise localization of the tumor border is therefore crucial for a successful surgery.
elements.sent-2411-022: During surgery, pre-operative MRI (pMRI) images are often used in a neuronavigation system, where the images are registered to the patients position on the operating table.
elements.sent-2411-023: An optically tracked tool, such as a pointer, can be used so that the surgeon can see the corresponding position of the pointer on the patient and the pMRI images during surgery.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, due to inaccuracies in the pMRI-to-patient registration and subsequent brain shift, the position of the pointer shown on the pMRI images cannot be fully trusted.
elements.sent-2411-025: Brain shift refers to tissue displacements due to pressure changes, edema, resection or other factors resulting in a mismatch between the image and the real position of the patient \cite{gerard_brain_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Intra-operative ultrasound imaging (iUS) can be used to bypass this problem as it is acquired directly in the frame of reference of the patient and does not depend on any registrations.
elements.sent-2411-027: Ultrasound is a low cost and portable imaging modality that gives a real-time image of the patient, and has shown to enhance the surgical outcome \cite{cepeda_non-navigated_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Intra-operative ultrasound images gives thus an updated and reliable image of the localization of the tumor.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the images have a limited field of view and often contain noise and artifacts that make them challenging to interpret \cite{carton_image_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Automatic brain tumor segmentation in intra-operative ultrasound images could facilitate the interpretation of the images and the localization of the tumor tissue, and thus help the surgeon perform a more complete resection.
elements.sent-2411-031: In addition, precise tumor annotations in the iUS image can be used to improve the MRI-to-patient registration algorithm to correct for brain shift during surgery.
elements.sent-2411-032: This segmentation task was the objective of the Correction of Brain Shift with Intra-Operative Ultrasound Segmentation (CuRIOUS-SEG) challenge in 2022 \cite{xiao_lesion_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The training set consisted of 23 3D ultrasound images from the RESECT \cite{xiao_retrospective_2017 dataset with annotations from the RESECT-SEG \cite{behboodi_resect-seg_2022 dataset, and the test set comprised 6 annotated 3D ultrasound images.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Three papers were published after this challenge, by Qayyum et al. \cite{qayyum_segmentation_2023 , Weld et al. \cite{weld_ultrasound_2023 and Sharifzadeh et al. \cite{sharifzadeh_segmentation_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Qayyum et al. \cite{qayyum_segmentation_2023 obtained the best results with an average Dice score of 0.57 obtained on the test set with a self-supervised learning-based 3DResUNet model.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Moreover, all papers had difficulties with certain cases.
elements.sent-2411-037: Considering the small size of the training set, poor generalization is not unexpected.
elements.sent-2411-038: Nevertheless, the RESECT dataset, with the RESECT-SEG annotations is, to date, the only publicly available annotated dataset.
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently, Dorent et al. \cite{dorent_miccai tried a new approach for this task with a patient-specific segmentation model trained on simulated ultrasound images from labeled pre-operative MRI images from a single patient.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Their approach obtain a median Dice score of 0.84-0.87 depending on the ground truth annotations when evaluated on a different 2D test set from 6 patients.
elements.sent-2411-041: In addition, they achieved better results by using the patient-specific model instead of training a general segmentation model using the RESECT dataset, where a median Dice score of 0.59-0.72 was obtained.
elements.sent-2411-042: On the other hand, this approach would only work for brain tumor segmentation before resection, and it is difficult to extend the model to include segmentation during and after resection when it is only trained on pre-resection situations.
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, a model has to be trained for each patient which is computationally extensive and time-consuming, and may thus not be possible in a clinical context.
elements.sent-2411-044: Having a good general model that works for all patients, would be simpler and more advantageous in a clinical context.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, as discussed, the lack of a large annotated US dataset limits the performances of the current general models.
elements.sent-2411-046: To address this issue, we investigated whether tumor annotations in pre-operative MRI images, could be used as a substitute for manual tumor delineations in ultrasound images to enlarge the training set of a deep learning model for automatic brain tumor segmentation in intra-operative ultrasound images.
elements.sent-2411-047: MRI tumor annotations are more accessible than US tumor annotation, either from published datasets\cite{juvekar_remind_2023 , or by well-performing tumor segmentation software, like Raidionics \cite{bouget_raidionics_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Before using the MRI tumor annotations as labels, they were transformed to align with the corresponding ultrasound image space by image registration.
elements.sent-2411-049: Since non-navigated 2D ultrasound imaging is more accessible and low-cost than advanced and navigated 3D ultrasound imaging included in a technologically advanced and high-cost system \cite{cepeda_non-navigated_2024 , the 3D images and labels were sliced up to 2D images to focus on segmentation models for 2D iUS images.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Training sets with only MRI tumor annotations, only US tumor annotations and with both MRI and US tumor annotations, were used to train a standardized deep learning model for medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-051: All models were tested and evaluated against a separate test set with published manual tumor annotations in iUS, to compare the difference between the different label origins.
elements.sent-2411-052: In addition, the models were compared to a measured inter-observer variability obtained in this study on the same test set.
elements.sent-2411-053: As a starting point for this novel method, only pre-resection iUS images were used with MRI tumor labels from pre-operative MRI images of patients who underwent surgery for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-054: For this project, annotated and un-annotated intra-operative ultrasound images were used.
elements.sent-2411-055: These were divided into two categories, namely US annotated data and MRI annotated data, as explained in Figure \ref{fig:data .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The US annotated data consists of intra-operative ultrasound images with manual tumor annotations, while the MRI annotated data comprise intra-operative ultrasound images without manual tumor annotations, but with pre-operative MRI images with tumor annotations.

elements.sent-2411-090: The open-source nnU-Net framework \cite{isensee_nnu-net_2021 was chosen in this study because it is a standardized and self-configuring deep learning framework where optimal pre-processing, hyper-parameters and post-processing are determined based on the given dataset.}

elements.subsection-2411-004: MRI annotated data: Comparison of different tumor area cut-off values
elements.sent-2411-098: In this experiment, we used the intra-operative ultrasound images with the MRI pseudo labels obtained from the MRI annotated data explained in Figure \ref{fig:data .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Since the images were obtained from slicing 3D volumes, the 2D slices contains tumors with a vast variability in tumor areas as seen in Figure \ref{fig:data_slice .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Slices from the edges of the tumor volumes are particularly small, and there is a higher risk of mismatch between the MRI pseudo label and the tumor in the iUS due to inaccurate image registration, compared to slices in the middle of the tumor volume with a larger tumor area.
elements.sent-2411-101: This experiment therefore studied different tumor area cut-off values in the MRI annotated data, to investigate the effect of excluding small and possibly poor aligned tumor labels that could be a source of noise rather than a valuable contribution to the training data.
elements.sent-2411-102: In addition, segmentation of small structures is a well-known problem in deep learning segmentation tasks, and excluding smaller structures, even with correct labels, could improve the models.
elements.sent-2411-103: Nine models were trained in this experiment with different tumor area cut-off values measured in square millimeters.
elements.sent-2411-104: Figure \ref{fig:dataMRI shows the model names and the number of training samples for each of these models.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The first part of the model name is based on the label origin, thus "MRI" for this experiment.
elements.sent-2411-106: The second part of the name shows the tumor area cut-off value in square millimeters.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus the MRI\_0 model is trained on all slices with a tumor larger than 0 mm$^2$, MRI\_5 on slices with a tumor area larger than 5 mm$^2$ and so on up to MRI\_300 which is trained on slices with a tumor area larger than 300 mm$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-108: All models were tested on all tumor-containing slices of the 29 3D volumes of the US annotated data.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{Fig4.pdf} \caption{The number of training samples for each model trained with MRI annotated data with different tumor area cut-off values.} \label{fig:dataMRI}
elements.subsection-2411-005: MRI and US annotated data: Comparison between MRI labels, US labels and manual annotations
elements.sent-2411-109: This experiment aimed to evaluate whether MRI tumor annotations can be used as a substitute for US tumor annotations to train a brain tumor segmentation model for ultrasound images.
elements.sent-2411-110: To do so, the MRI\_200 model was compared to US\_200, a model trained on only US annotated data, and MRI+US\_200, a model trained on both US and MRI annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-111: In addition, the predictions of the deep learning models were compared to manual annotations validated by an expert neuro-surgeon for this project on the same test set.
elements.sent-2411-112: The US annotated data was split into a training and test set, where the 23 patients from RESECT were used for training and the 6 patients from the test set of the CuRIOUS-SEG challenge were used for testing of all models to ensure a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-113: Based on the results from the first experiments, 8 3D images from the MRI annotated data were excluded from the training set of the MRI+US\_200 model due to poor image quality.
elements.sent-2411-114: To ensure a reasonable comparison between the models, all models were trained using nnU-Net with the same tumor area cut-off of 200 mm$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-115: This was chosen based on the results from the first experiment.
elements.sent-2411-117: The inter-observer variability of manual intra-operative ultrasound segmentation was measured between two expert annotators, to assess the difficulty of the task.
elements.sent-2411-118: To do so, the first author manually annotated 3D images from the same 6 patients from the test set of CuRIOUS-SEG, which was also used as test set in the second experiment in this project, using 3D Slicer (Version 5.2.2) \cite{fedorov_3d_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: The annotations were adjusted and validated by an experienced neurosurgeon (OS) before they were sliced to 2D and compared against the sliced published ground truth segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-120: In the following, "Annotator" is referring to the results from this.
elements.sent-2411-121: To evaluate different aspects of the pixel-wise segmentation performance of each model, average Dice score, precision, recall and false negative rate (FNR) were computed between the published ground truth masks and the binary prediction masks on the test set for each experiment.
elements.sent-2411-122: Since the number of training samples changed for every model, a separate test set was used to ensure a fair comparison on unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-123: In addition, the percentage of positive predictions (PP) are calculated image-wise.

elements.sent-2411-135: This paper studied whether manual and time-consuming US tumor annotations can be replaced by MRI tumor annotations to expand the training set for an automatic brain tumor segmentation model for 2D intra-operative ultrasound images.
elements.sent-2411-136: The nnU-Net framework was used to study this by training models with different training datasets and different label origins.
elements.sent-2411-137: First, the models were trained using only registered MRI tumor annotations as labels with different tumor area cut-off values to find the best configuration of the training data.
elements.sent-2411-138: The best performing model was compared to models trained with only US annotated tumors and both US and MRI annotated tumors, and to expert-validated manual annotations, to evaluate the use of MRI tumor annotations.
elements.sent-2411-139: The results from the first experiment indicate that the largest difference between the models trained with different tumor area cut-off values, was their ability to detect the tumor, not segmentation performance.
elements.sent-2411-140: The MRI\_200 model and MRI\_300 model found a tumor in 98.6 \% and 99.8 \% of the test slices, respectively, while the number of predicted positive cases decreased for decreasing tumor area cut-off values to 73 \% for the MRI\_0 model.

elements.sent-2411-207: Automatic segmentation of brain tumors in intra-operative ultrasound (iUS) images could facilitate localization of tumor tissue during resection surgery.
elements.sent-2411-208: In this paper, we investigated the use of tumor annotations in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans, which are more accessible than annotations in iUS images, for training of deep learning models for iUS brain tumor segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-209: We used 180 annotated MRI scans with corresponding unannotated iUS images, and 29 annotated iUS images.
elements.sent-2411-210: Image registration was performed to transfer the MRI annotations to the corresponding iUS images before training the nnU-Net model with different configurations of the data and label origins.
elements.sent-2411-211: The results showed no significant difference in Dice score for a model trained with only MRI annotated tumors compared to models trained with only iUS annotations and both, and to expert annotations, indicating that MRI tumor annotations can be used as a substitute for iUS tumor annotations to train a deep learning model for automatic brain tumor segmentation in iUS images.
elements.sent-2411-212: The best model obtained an average Dice score of $0.62\pm0.31$, compared to $0.67\pm0.25$ for an expert neurosurgeon, where the performance on larger tumors were similar, but lower for the models on smaller tumors.
elements.sent-2411-213: In addition, the results showed that removing smaller tumors from the training sets improved the results.
elements.sent-2411-214: The main models are available here: \url{https://github.com/mathildefaanes/us_brain_tumor_segmentation/tree/main.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Diffuse gliomas are primary brain tumors that infiltrate normal brain tissue, making complete surgical resection impossible \cite{delgado-lopez_diffuse_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: Although incurable, treatment can prolong life and improve quality of life and brain functions \cite{kheirollahi_brain_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-217: Surgical resection is often the preferred primary treatment option, as the extent of tumor resection is linked to prolonged survival \cite{sanai_glioma_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: However, removing or damaging healthy surrounding tissue can negatively impact patient function \cite{sastry_applications_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Precise tumor border localization is therefore crucial for successful resection.
elements.sent-2411-220: Low-cost, real-time intra-operative ultrasound (iUS) has been shown to enhance the surgical outcome \cite{cepeda_non-navigated_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-221: However, the images have a limited field of view and often contain noise and artifacts that make them difficult to interpret \cite{carton_image_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-222: Automatic brain tumor segmentation in iUS images could facilitate interpretation and help the surgeon perform a more complete resection.
elements.sent-2411-223: Deep learning can address this segmentation task as demonstrated in the CuRIOUS-SEG challenge in 2022 \cite{xiao_lesion_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-224: The winning contribution came from Qayyum et al. \cite{qayyum_segmentation_2023 who achieved a Dice score of 0.57 using 23 annotated iUS images from RESECT \cite{xiao_retrospective_2017 and RESECT-SEG \cite{behboodi_resect-seg_2022 as training set, which are to date, the only publicly available annotated dataset.}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Recently, Dorent et al. \cite{dorent_miccai introduced a patient-specific segmentation model trained on simulated ultrasound images from pre-operative MRI scans from a single patient.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Their approach obtained a median Dice score of 0.84-0.87, compared to 58.5-71.7 for a general model trained on the RESECT dataset \cite{dorent_miccai .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Despite good results, their approach is limited to pre-resection scenarios and requires time-consuming and computationally extensive training for each patient.
elements.sent-2411-228: Having a model that generalizes well to all patients would be more advantageous in the clinic, but the lack of large annotated iUS datasets limits further development.
elements.sent-2411-229: To address this issue, we explored whether tumor annotations in pre-operative MRI scans could be used as a substitute for manual tumor delineations in iUS images to expand the training set of a deep learning model for automatic brain tumor segmentation in iUS images.
elements.sent-2411-230: MRI tumor annotations are more accessible than iUS tumor annotations, either from published datasets \cite{juvekar_remind_2023 , or by software for automatic tumor segmentation such as Raidionics \cite{bouget_raidionics_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-231: Since non-navigated 2D ultrasound imaging is more commonly used and low-cost than 3D ultrasound imaging \cite{cepeda_non-navigated_2024 , this work focuses on 2D images.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Training sets with only MRI tumor annotations, only iUS tumor annotations and both MRI and iUS tumor annotation were used to train 2D deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-233: All models were evaluated on a separate test set with manual iUS annotations, and compared to the inter-observer variability.

elements.sent-2411-255: The nnU-Net framework was used in this study because it is a standardized and self-configuring deep learning framework, where optimal pre-processing, hyperparameters and post-processing are determined based on the given dataset.

elements.sent-2411-302: The purpose of this study was to investigate whether MRI tumor annotations can successfully be used as a replacement for iUS tumor annotations, for training an automatic brain tumor segmentation model for iUS images.
elements.sent-2411-303: In the first experiment, we found that the models' performance improves by increasing tumor area cut-off values in the training set as evidenced by a significant increase in Dice score, recall and PP, and a significant decrease in FNR ($P<0.0064$, Bonferroni) compared to using all tumor areas (MRI\_0), shown in Fig. \ref{fig:Res-tumorarea-MRI and Table \ref{tab:MRI_tumor_area_test_set .}}
elements.sent-2411-304: Precision remains unchanged ($P>0.0064$, Bonferroni), suggesting that the ability to detect tumor pixels improves with larger tumor area cut-off values.
elements.sent-2411-305: This is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:mri_ex , showing similar segmentation masks for all models except the MRI\_0 and MRI\_35 models where the tumor is missed in some cases.}
elements.sent-2411-306: In addition, we found that a tumor area cut-off value around 200 mm$^2$ seems to provide the best and most stable results as indicated by that improvement in Dice score stagnates with a tumor cut-off value at 200 mm$^2$, regardless of tumor area in the test set, and by that the MRI\_200 model has the highest effect size, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:Res-tumorarea-MRI and Table \ref{tab:MRI_tumor_area_test_set .}}
elements.sent-2411-307: This value was therefore used in the second experiment.
elements.sent-2411-308: A possible explanation for the improvement in model performance when excluding the smallest tumors from the training set could be that the inaccuracies in the image registration have a larger impact on the smaller tumors, and including these might add more noise to the training data rather than valuable information.
elements.sent-2411-309: This illustrates that higher quality over quantity in the training data might be a good compromise in this case, as including the smaller tumors increases the training sample size, but decreases the performance.
elements.sent-2411-310: This can be seen in Fig. \ref{fig:mri_ex .}
elements.sent-2411-311: Additionally, segmentation of small structures is a well-known challenge in medical image segmentation because the amount of tumor pixels is low compared to background pixels, giving a high class-imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-312: Furthermore, Dice score, which is used in the loss function of nnU-Net combined with cross-entropy, is highly sensitive to errors in small structures \cite{isensee_nnu-net_2021 , and including small structures in the training could be disadvantageous for the network update.}
elements.sent-2411-313: From the results in the second experiment, we found indistinguishable performances from models trained with different label origins, evidenced by similar scores and no significant differences (P$>0.0085$, Bonferroni) and very small effect sizes (d$\sim0.01$), as shown in Table \ref{tab:MRI_tumor_area_test_set , Fig. \ref{fig:comp-res and Table \ref{tab:cohen_P .}}}
elements.sent-2411-314: The average Dice scores were $0.62\pm0.31$, $0.59\pm0.28$, and $0.58\pm0.32$ for the MRI+US\_200, US\_200, and MRI\_200 model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-315: This indicates that MRI tumor annotation can be used as labels for ultrasound images.
elements.sent-2411-316: As a results, clinicians can save valuable time by reducing time spent on manually annotating ultrasound images for developing deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-317: However, the results did not improve even though the MRI+US\_200 model was trained on a dataset approximately seven times larger compared to the US\_200 models.
elements.sent-2411-318: Table \ref{tab:MRI_tumor_area_test_set and Fig. \ref{fig:seg_exp show that the models in this experiment also have poor results on small tumors, which could indicate that small tumors limit further improvements.}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Regarding the comparison of the deep learning models' performance to the Annotator's performance, the Annotator achieved superior scores for all metrics on the entire test set, although no statistically significant differences were found (P$>0.0085$, Bonferroni), and the effect sizes were small (d$\sim0.01$).
elements.sent-2411-320: However, the statistical analysis was limited by the size of the test set.
elements.sent-2411-321: Although the number of 2D slices was large, the small patient sample (n=6) makes the performance highly sensitive to the test patients.
elements.sent-2411-322: A larger test set with diverse tumor types, annotated by multiple experts, would be in favor of a more robust statistical analysis, and better evaluation of the models.
elements.sent-2411-323: Nevertheless, the Dice scores obtained on the larger tumors ($>$ 200 mm$^2$) were similar to the scores achieved by the deep learning models, indicating an expert-level performance for larger tumors.
elements.sent-2411-324: However the segmentation of small structures remains a challenge, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:seg_exp .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Compared to previous work, Qayyum et al. \cite{qayyum_segmentation_2023 obtained an average Dice score of 0.57 on the same test set in 3D, compared to 0.62 for the MRI+US\_200 model in 2D.}
elements.sent-2411-326: The patient-specific approach of Dorent et al. \cite{dorent_miccai , achieved better results with a median Dice score of 0.84-0.87, compared to a median Dice score of 0.74 for the MRI+US\_200 model.}
elements.sent-2411-327: However, these models were fine tuned to each patient in the training dataset, requiring an amount of time and resources that makes a clinical adaption of the models challenging.
elements.sent-2411-328: Despite promising results using MRI tumor annotations as a substitute for iUS annotations, the task of intra-operative brain tumor segmentation remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-329: Although the models achieved promising performances on larger tumors, more thorough testing and validation is needed before clinical use.
elements.sent-2411-330: In addition, the detection of small structures remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-331: It is crucial for extending the model to segmentation of tumor tissue during and after resection, and should be the focus of future work.
elements.sent-2411-332: An overall limitation of this study is the small number of patients.
elements.sent-2411-333: Tumor annotations in both pre- and post-operative MRI scans from patients who have undergone brain tumor resection surgery, with corresponding iUS images, can be included to increase the number of smaller tumors in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-334: Different methods for improving the registration of the MRI labels to the iUS tumor, such as affine or non-linear registration methods or manual adjustment, could be explored.
elements.sent-2411-335: Additionally, loss functions tailored for small structures, a fundamentally different deep learning network like a vision transformer network, or foundation models like USFM \cite{jiao_usfm_2024 , could be tested.}
elements.sent-2411-336: Other strategies than semantic segmentation could also be investigated, such as bounding boxes proposed by Weld et.. al \cite{weld_challenges_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Although the detection of small tumors remains a challenge, our study showed that MRI tumor annotations can successfully be used as substitute labels for unannotated iUS images to train an automatic brain tumor segmentation model for 2D iUS images.
